candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: This is now the top open frontier family once the absorbed restore/export families and the rejected handoff sub-variant are filtered out.
The restored PTX surface remains the overall best branch, so the next recommendation should stay on that branch rather than resetting immediately to an alternate surface.
It ranks first because it is the highest-confidence unabsorbed follow-on on the current winner.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 14 restored the PTX winner after the round-13 handoff miss and confirmed that the regression was a one-variant failure rather than wider source drift: the current source now matches the best-known `489574e` kernel file exactly and runtime recovered from 24.48483181 ms back to 24.17859173 ms. That also means several families should be filtered out again for this round: the restore family is absorbed on the current source, the export-cleanup family is already baked into this PTX surface, and the A-then-B handoff variant is now a measured negative. With those filters applied, the strongest remaining live family is the active PTX control path. The branch still runs at only 16.62% active warps with non-trivial barrier and long-scoreboard cost, so one more bounded control-path exploit remains the best next move as long as it does not replay the already-tested B-first prologue, issue-grouping, or A-then-B handoff sub-variants.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual control-path and consume-order overhead inside the active PTX hot-band microkernel after the absorbed restore/export cleanup and the rejected handoff variant are removed from consideration.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The family is still the best active-branch lever, but prior sub-variants have mixed evidence, so another attempt must stay narrowly distinct from the already-closed control-order and handoff changes.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.178592 ms accepted base and the 24.164272 ms best-known run
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the next control-path tweak replays any already-measured prologue, issue-grouping, or handoff sub-variant, or if it remains runtime-flat on the restored PTX surface.

candidates.direction_name: Restore accepted grouped_rows=8 hot-band consumer ordering
ranking_notes: This stays near the top because the user explicitly asked to keep promising round-history states active in the live queue.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the grouped_rows=4 PTX surface remains the best measured branch and has not been exhausted yet.
Keeping it second preserves a real alternate PTX surface if active-branch control tuning flattens again.
candidates.hypothesis: The loop should keep one alternate PTX surface alive in the live queue in case the grouped_rows=4 branch saturates again after the restore. The grouped_rows=8 consumer-ordering family remains the strongest such fallback. It is still open in the frontier, still comes from measured round-history recovery, and still gives the search a materially different PTX locality regime. It stays second because the restored grouped_rows=4 PTX surface remains the best measured branch when the handoff order is correct.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Consumer-side ordering and grouped-row locality inside the PTX hot-band microkernel under the grouped_rows=8 regime.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is an alternate-branch reset onto a historically slower PTX surface, so it should remain a fallback until the grouped_rows=4 control family is exhausted or clearly stalls.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.178592 ms accepted base and the 24.164272 ms best-known run
correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the grouped_rows=8 restore remains materially behind the restored grouped_rows=4 PTX surface without showing a clearly better scheduler signature.

candidates.direction_name: Retune The Auxiliary 256x128 Hot-Band K-Loop Schedule
ranking_notes: This family stays in the set to preserve one non-PTX geometry fallback backed by round-history evidence.
It ranks below the two PTX directions because the active PTX surface still owns the best measured runtime.
Keeping it third avoids queue collapse while still respecting the current active-branch evidence.
candidates.hypothesis: The queue still needs one orthogonal non-PTX fallback from `round_history` so the search does not collapse entirely into PTX-local variants. The auxiliary 256x128 hot-band family remains the cleanest such fallback because it already carries async staging and a larger reuse tile, yet still exposes a bounded local knob in the K-loop schedule. It stays third because the immediate priority is still to work the active PTX surface, but it remains worth carrying as a real geometry-side escape hatch.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Compute scheduling and latency hiding on the auxiliary 256x128 hot-band path, not DRAM bandwidth and not another PTX grouped-row retime.
candidates.risk: Medium. This branch is orthogonal and reversible, but it is not the current default, so upside depends on the alternate geometry becoming more competitive after a bounded schedule cleanup.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 schedule retune cannot get meaningfully closer to the 24.16-24.18 ms band or if it reopens a broad geometry regression.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
